provisions.title: IAIS Application Paper on Group-Wide Supervision
provisions.text: The IAIS application paper on group-wide supervision provides guidance on: the identification of the group and its legal and management structure; the role of the group-wide supervisor and host supervisors; information sharing and cooperation through supervisory colleges; the assessment of group-wide risks (intra-group transactions, contagion risk, concentration risk, strategic and reputational risk); the review of group governance and enterprise risk management; and the application of the ICS as the group-wide capital requirement. The paper also addresses challenges of supervising insurance groups with complex cross-border structures.

provisions.title: IAIS Application Paper on Conduct of Business
provisions.text: The IAIS application paper on the supervision of conduct of business covers: product design and approval (product governance, target market identification); selling practices (suitability, disclosure, advice standards); claims handling (fair and timely settlement, policyholder communication); complaint handling (accessible procedures, root cause analysis); and culture and governance (tone from the top, conduct risk frameworks, remuneration alignment). The paper emphasises that conduct supervision should be proportionate and risk-based, with particular attention to vulnerable consumers.

provisions.title: IAIS Application Paper on Macroprudential Supervision
provisions.text: The IAIS application paper on macroprudential supervision covers: the identification and assessment of systemic risk in the insurance sector; liquidity risk management (stressed liquidity scenarios, contingency funding plans); counterparty exposure management (large exposure limits, reinsurance credit risk); recovery and resolution planning (recovery plans, resolution strategies, policyholder protection schemes); and macroprudential surveillance (global monitoring exercise, data collection, cross-sectoral analysis). The paper provides practical guidance on implementing the holistic framework for systemic risk in insurance.

provisions.title: IAIS Guidance on Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA)
provisions.text: The IAIS provides guidance on the Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA) that insurers should conduct. The ORSA should include: identification of all material risks (including risks not captured by the regulatory capital requirement); assessment of the insurer's overall solvency needs (using internal models, stress testing, or other methods); projection of the insurer's financial position over the business planning horizon; assessment of the quality and adequacy of capital resources; and review of the insurer's risk management and governance processes. The ORSA should be proportionate to the nature, scale, and complexity of the insurer's business.

provisions.title: IAIS Guidance on Reinsurance and Other Risk Transfer Mechanisms
provisions.text: The IAIS provides guidance on the supervision of reinsurance and other risk transfer mechanisms. Key areas include: assessment of the effectiveness of risk transfer (genuine risk transfer vs. financing arrangements); counterparty credit risk from reinsurance (credit quality of reinsurers, collateralisation requirements); concentration risk in reinsurance programmes; supervision of cross-border reinsurance; and alternative risk transfer mechanisms (insurance-linked securities, catastrophe bonds, industry loss warranties). Supervisors should assess the adequacy of risk transfer when evaluating insurers' capital positions.

provisions.title: IAIS Guidance on Insurance Sector Operational Resilience
provisions.text: The IAIS application paper on operational resilience covers: identification of critical business services (underwriting, claims, investment management); impact tolerance setting (maximum acceptable disruption to critical services); scenario testing (disruption scenarios including cyber attacks, natural disasters, pandemic); third-party dependency management (critical outsourcing, cloud services); communication and coordination (with policyholders, regulators, and market participants during disruption); and self-assessment and remediation. Insurers should demonstrate they can remain within impact tolerances for critical business services under severe but plausible disruption scenarios.

provisions.title: IAIS Issues Paper on Digitalisation in Insurance
provisions.text: The IAIS has published an issues paper on the impact of digitalisation on the insurance sector. Key areas include: usage-based and parametric insurance products (telematics, IoT sensors, smart contracts); digital distribution channels (comparison websites, aggregators, embedded insurance); artificial intelligence in underwriting and claims (automated risk assessment, fraud detection); blockchain and distributed ledger technology (smart contracts, claims automation); RegTech and SupTech (automated compliance, supervisory data analytics); and data ethics (algorithmic fairness, privacy, discrimination prevention).

provisions.title: IADI Enhanced Guidance on Public Awareness
provisions.text: IADI enhanced guidance on public awareness covers: strategies for ensuring depositors understand their coverage (coverage levels, eligible products, excluded products); communication channels (websites, social media, financial literacy programmes, branch materials); coordination with member institutions (required notices, disclosure at account opening); crisis communication plans (rapid communication during bank failure events); and measurement of awareness effectiveness (surveys, awareness tracking, evaluation of information retention). Deposit insurers should establish target awareness levels and regularly measure progress.

provisions.title: IADI Enhanced Guidance on Coverage Policy
provisions.text: IADI enhanced guidance on coverage policy covers: setting coverage levels (balancing moral hazard reduction with depositor protection, typically covering 90-95% of depositors in full); eligible deposit products (demand deposits, savings, term deposits, foreign currency deposits); excluded products (interbank deposits, insider deposits, government deposits); coverage per depositor per institution (aggregation rules); temporary high-balance coverage (life events such as property sales, inheritance, insurance payouts); and coordination of coverage across deposit insurance schemes in countries with multiple schemes.

provisions.title: IADI Enhanced Guidance on Resolution
provisions.text: IADI enhanced guidance on the role of deposit insurers in resolution covers: the powers and tools available to deposit insurers (payout, purchase and assumption, bridge bank support, open bank assistance); the coordination framework between deposit insurers and resolution authorities; least-cost resolution principles (the deposit insurer's contribution should not exceed the cost of a payout); loss-sharing arrangements in resolution; and cross-border coordination (recognition of foreign resolution actions, cooperation with foreign deposit insurers). The guidance also addresses the treatment of deposits in bail-in.

provisions.title: IADI Core Principles for Islamic Deposit Insurance Systems
provisions.text: IADI has published supplementary guidance on the application of core principles to Islamic deposit insurance systems. Key considerations include: Shariah-compliant deposit products eligible for coverage (wadiah, mudarabah, qard); the distinction between deposit-like products (profit-sharing investment accounts) and conventional deposits; Shariah governance of the deposit insurance fund (investment in halal instruments, prohibition of riba); payout mechanisms that comply with Shariah principles; and the treatment of profit-sharing investment accounts (whether they qualify as deposits for insurance purposes).

provisions.title: IADI Cross-Border Deposit Insurance Issues
provisions.text: IADI has published guidance on cross-border deposit insurance issues. Key considerations include: home-host arrangements (whether deposits at foreign branches are covered by the home or host scheme); topping-up arrangements (host scheme providing supplementary coverage above home scheme levels); information sharing between deposit insurance authorities (depositor data, failure scenarios); coordination of payout across jurisdictions (timing, currency, communication); and single customer view (SCV) data standards for cross-border banking groups. Deposit insurers should establish cooperation agreements with their foreign counterparts.
